segments.s133-g25: چو is the comparative particle 'like/as'. It introduces a simile: proper-name گرگین (Gorgin) is followed by و 'and' and a repeated چو 'like/as' before زنگه; شاوران is a plural noun 'counselors' that states the role. The copula is omitted (typical in poetry), so the sequence reads 'like Gorgin and like Zangeh, (they were) counselors.'
segments.s133-g26: Again چو begins a simile 'like/as'. گستهم here is being used as a proper name (parallel to گرگین above) rather than its literal sense; و 'and' links the two names. خراد functions as an adjective 'wise' modifying the following plural جنگ‌آوران 'warriors'. The noun phrase means 'like Gostaham and (the) wise warriors.'
segments.s133-g27: چو 'like/as' introduces another simile; برزین is a proper name (Borzin) followed by the compound epithet گردنکش 'head‑strong/rebellious' and the noun تیغ‌زن 'swordsman/attacker'. The epithets follow the name in apposition, forming 'like Borzin, the headstrong swordsman.'
segments.s133-g28: This line is a short interrogative with an omitted copula (typical in Persian poetry). گرازه 'boar/sow' is the subject, کجا 'where' is the interrogative/adverb, and بد سر انجمن literally 'bad‑headed in the assembly' functions as the predicate ('a poor/ill leader in the assembly'). In modern English we render it as a rhetorical question: 'where was the boar a bad head of the assembly?'.
segments.s133-g29: هر یک از ... is the standard circumstantial phrase 'each one of ...'. مهتران is the plural 'chiefs/leaders' (here 'each of the commanders') and مرد چند means 'several men'. The line is a nominal clause describing possession/retinue; the finite verb is again omitted as is common in poetic enumeration.
segments.s133-g30: یکی here functions as 'one' or 'each one' (= 'each had one...') and modifies لشکری 'troop/host'. The adjectives نامدار 'renowned' and ارجمند 'esteemed/honored' follow the noun in typical Persian order, together qualifying the troop. The phrase reads 'each (had) a renowned, honored troop.'
segments.s133-g31: نیاسود is a negative perfect/participial form meaning 'did not rest/was not at ease'; it predicates over لشکر 'the host/army'. زمانی 'for a while' qualifies the duration, and ز کار 'from work/effort' gives the source/reason. The verb is expressed by the participle نیاسود rather than a separate finite verb, a common poetic construction.
segments.s133-g32: This is a list introduced by the preposition ز 'from' (poetic for 'from/with respect to') followed by leisure and martial pastimes: چوگان (polo), تیر (arrow/archery), نبید (wine), and شکار (the hunt). The sequence exemplifies activities from which the host did not take rest; items are connected with و 'and'.
segments.s133-g17: به جایی کجا = a prepositional phrase 'to a place where'; به (preposition) + جایی (noun 'place') + کجا (relative 'where') form 'to a place where...'. نام او = 'his name / his reputation' (noun + possessive). بد نوند is a poetic/archaic phrase: بد (bad/ill) qualifies نوند, here functioning adjectivally/verbally to mean 'was ill-sounding / infamous' — the verb is compressed into the adjective phrase typical of classical Persian verse.
segments.s133-g18: بدو اندرون کاخهای بلند = a directional phrase 'unto him into the inner parts of the lofty palaces'. بدو (to him/unto him) is the dative clitic phrase; اندرون (inside/inner quarters) gives the internal location; کاخهای بلند is the noun phrase 'the lofty palaces' (کاخ + plural ezāfe-like construction), placed after the locative to satisfy the poem's word order.
segments.s133-g19: کجا آذر تیز برزین کنون = 'where Azar‑Tiz Barzin now (is/acts)'. کجا introduces the relative clause 'where'; آذر تیز برزین is a name with an epithet (آذر 'Azar' used as part of the name or epithet, تیز 'swift/keen', and برزین the proper name); کنون is the adverb 'now' placed after the name for emphasis, a normal position in classical Persian.
segments.s133-g20: بدانجا فروزد همی رهنمون = 'to that place he lights (or illuminates) continually the guide/way.' بدانجا (to that place) is a directional adverb; فروزد is a poetic present/preterite form of فروزیدن (to kindle/shine) used here transitively/intransitively in the sense 'lights / illuminates' (metaphorically 'leads'); همی is the habitual/continuous adverb 'always/continually'; رهنمون 'guidance/guide' functions as the object or the thing illuminated — together giving 'he continually lights/illuminates the way to that place.'
segments.s133-g21: بزرگان ایران بدان بزمگاه = 'the great men of Iran to that banquet‑hall'. بزرگان ایران is the subject ('the great/noble men of Iran'); بدان بزمگاه is a locative phrase ('to that bāzm-gāh / banquet‑hall') formed from the prepositional prefix ب + دان (classical deictic) + بزمگاه (banquet‑hall). Word order places the locative after the subject as is common in poetry.
segments.s133-g22: شدند انجمن نامور یک سپاه = 'they became an assembly, renowned, a single host.' شدند is the past/plural perfective 'they became' or 'they were formed'; انجمن نامور is the predicative noun phrase 'a renowned assembly' (انجمن 'assembly', نامور 'renowned'); یک سپاه is an appositive 'one host' or 'a single host', completing the idea that they formed a great host/assembly.
segments.s133-g23: چو طوس و چو گودرز کشوادگان = a series introduced by چو 'like/as' before each name. چو طوس و چو گودرز = 'as Tous and as Goudarz'. کشوادگان is a descriptive/epithet attached to these names (an archaic group-name/epithet in the manuscript); the line lists leaders by name followed by their group epithet, a common poetic device.
segments.s133-g24: چو بهرام و چون گیو آزادگان = a parallel clause continuing the list: چو/چون 'like' + Bahram and Giv; آزادگان is the plural epithet 'the free, the noble ones'. The stanza uses repeated چو/چون to coordinate names and then gives the collective epithet.
segments.s133-g9: چو is the conditional/time conjunction 'when' (classical use) that sets up the clause; همره here functions adverbially ('with, as companion') and precedes the verb کنی (2nd person singular subjunctive/imperative-ish form of کردن) — together 'چو ... کنی' = 'when you do / when you take'. جنگ را is the definite direct object marked by را, and با خرد is a prepositional phrase 'with wisdom' indicating accompaniment or means.
segments.s133-g10: دلیرت is a possessed noun ('your valour/your brave quality') with the enclitic -ت. ز is the preposition 'from/among' (poetic z), taking جنگ‌آوران ('warriors') as its object. نشمرد is the negative simple form 'does not count/reckon' (3rd singular); grammatically the possessed noun (دلیرت) is the topic/subject and نشمرد predicates that it is not counted among the warriors.
segments.s133-g11: خرد را و دین را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker ('as for wisdom and as for religion'). رهی is an archaic/formal noun meaning 'a way/path' and stands before the copula دیگرست ('is different/other'); together 'رهی دیگرست' = 'the path is different'. The structure places the topics (خرد، دین) before the predicate.
segments.s133-g12: سخنهای نیکو is a noun phrase 'good/fine words' (sokanhā = plural). به بند اندرست is a locative/predicative phrase: به marks relation ('in/into/at'), بند here means 'stanza' (poetic unit), and اندرست (from اندر + است) is the copula 'is within/placed in'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'fine words are within the stanza.'
segments.s133-g13: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. از + ره is a source expression ('from the way/course/account of'), so از ره رستم جنگجوی means 'from the exploits/course of Rostam the warrior' — رستم modifies جنگجوی as a name with an epithet. از introduces the source, ره is the noun 'course/way'.
segments.s133-g14: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one'. داستانست is the fused form of داستان است ('it is a tale'); با introduces accompanying qualities, here رنگ و بوی ('colour and scent') — a figurative collocation meaning vividness/pleasantness. The clause states that 'a certain tale (is) with colour and scent' i.e. richly coloured/deliciously told.
segments.s133-g15: شنیدم که introduces indirect speech: 'I heard that ...'. روزی is the temporal adverb 'one day / once' (classical usage). گو here appears before the place-name پیلتن and functions idiomatically as 'called' or introduces the name (in older texts گو can mark a name); پیلتن is a place-name. The whole is 'I heard that one day (in) Piltan ...'.
segments.s133-g16: یکی is 'a certain' (indefinite). سور کرد is a past simple verb phrase 'held a feast/banquet' (سور = feast, کرد = did/made). از در انجمن is a locational/idiomatic phrase; از در can mean 'at/from the gate/at the court of', and انجمن is 'assembly/meeting (hall)', so the phrase indicates the feast took place in the assembly/meeting-hall.
segments.s133-g57: دگر سو is a two-word locative: دگر ("other") + سو ("side"), meaning "on the other side/elsewhere." سرخس و بیابانش is a coordinated noun phrase "ferns and its/wild open country" (بیابانش = بیابان + enclitic -ش 'its/of it'). پیش is an adverb/postposition meaning "before/ahead," placed after the nouns (poetic word-order inversion).
segments.s133-g58: گله گشته is a small clause where گله ("flock/herd") is followed by the verbal adjective/compound گشته (from گشت "become/turned") = "has become/formed into a flock." بر دشت is a locative phrase "on the plain," and آهو و میش names the animals ("gazelle(s) and ewes"). The verb گشته applies to the whole nominal phrase.
segments.s133-g59: همه دشت = "the whole/plain entire plain" (همه used as adjective "entire"). پر here is a stative adjective "full (of)" placed after the head noun (دشت پر خرگه و خیمه = "the plain full of stables and tents"). گشت is the past-inchoative verb "became/turned" applying to the preceding description.
segments.s133-g60: از انبوه آهو is an ablative/cause phrase "from/out of the multitude of gazelles;" سراسیمه گشت uses سراسیمه ("frantic, flustered") with گشت ("became") to say "it became frantic/was thrown into alarm." The subject is carried over from the previous clause (the plain), so the verb is short and elliptic as is common in poetry.
segments.s133-g61: ز (poetic form of از) marks cause/agent here: "because of/from the predatory lions." درنده modifies شیران: a pre-nominal adjective phrase "the predatory/ferocious lions." زمین شد تهی is a standard predicative clause "the land became empty" with شد + adjectival predicate تهی.
segments.s133-g62: به marks the indirect object/recipient: "to/for the flying birds." پرنده مرغان is a descriptive phrase "winged/flying birds" (پرنده qualifying مرغان). رسید آگهی is a verbal clause with رسید "arrived/reached" and آگهی "news/information," so literally "news reached the birds." Note the object (آگهی) follows the verb in this poetic ordering.
segments.s133-g63: تلی هر سویی is a locative expression "heaps/piles on every side" (تلی = "heap/pile", هر سویی = "on every side"). مرغ و نخجیر بود is an existential/predicative clause "there were birds and game" with بود as the past copula/existential "there was/were." Poetic order places the locative before the predicate.
segments.s133-g64: اگر introduces a conditional "if." The phrase can be read as a compact conditional: اگر کشته‌گر خسته ("if the slayer grew tired") تیر بود then follows as a consequence/statement about arrows: تیر بود ("the arrow(s) were/was [spent/exhausted]"). Poetic ellipsis allows the consequence to be terse; syntactically, بود is the past copula and تیر is the noun constituting the predicate/affected item. (The line is slightly ambiguous in isolation; this explanation gives the normal reading in context.)
segments.s133-g49: 'ببودند' is the archaic/past plural of بودن (they were). 'یکسر' is an adverb meaning 'entirely/altogether' modifying the clause. 'برین' here is formed from the preposition بر + the demonstrative/archaic element -ین and means 'on that matter' / 'in that respect'. 'هم‌سخن' is a predicative compound meaning 'of the same speech' = 'like‑minded'. Word order is typical older Persian: verb (ببودند) with adverbial and predicative elements surrounding it.
segments.s133-g50: 'کسی' here is indefinite ('anyone/no one' in negative contexts). 'رای' means 'opinion/counsel'. 'دیگر' qualifies رای as 'another/different'. 'نیفگند' is the negative past (نی- + افگند/افکندن) meaning 'did not cast/offer' — so 'did not offer/voice'. 'بن' is a short, somewhat archaic final particle here; in poetry it functions as a resumptive or emphatic word ('thereupon/then' or simply fills the meter). The whole segment means 'no one voiced another opinion.'
segments.s133-g51: 'سحرگه' is an adverbial noun meaning 'at dawn'. 'چو' is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. 'از خواب برخاستند' is a past plural clause: 'از' (from/out of) + 'خواب' (sleep) + 'برخاستند' (they rose); the verb appears at the end as usual. Together: 'When at dawn they rose from sleep.'
segments.s133-g52: 'بران' is an adverbial/relational particle meaning 'thereon/thence/for that' (literally 'on that'). 'آرزو' is the noun 'desire/wish' and names the motivating object. 'رفتن' is the infinitive 'to go' used here as the verbal noun indicating the action intended. 'آراستند' is past plural of آرایش‌دادن/آراستن meaning 'they made ready/arranged/adorned' — here 'آراستند' governs the infinitive رفتن and means 'they prepared to go' or 'they set out.' The sequence shows purpose/intent: they readied themselves to go because of that desire.
segments.s133-g53: 'برفتند' is past plural (they departed/they went off). 'با' is the preposition 'with'. The list 'باز و شاهین و مهد' enumerates companions/equipment: 'باز' (hawk/falcon), 'شاهین' (falcon), and 'مهد' — here 'مهد' is a noun placed in the list; in older poetry nouns can appear compactly without a linking particle. Functionally the segment means 'they went with hawk and falcon and [with] ...' (companions or hunting paraphernalia).
segments.s133-g54: 'گرازنده' is an agent noun 'boar‑hunter' (one who hunts boar). 'و شاد' adds 'and merry/joyful' describing the party. 'تا' here functions as a limitative/prepositional word meaning 'as far as / up to / toward' (i.e., indicating destination or extent). 'رود' is a noun 'river' and 'شهد' is 'honey' — together 'رودِ شهد' = 'the honeyed river/stream'. The clause means they went on, joyful, as far as the honeyed stream.
segments.s133-g55: 'به' is the directional preposition 'to/into'. 'نخچیرگاه' is a compound noun meaning 'hunting-ground'. 'رد' here functions as a noun 'passage/route' (or a poetic locational element) connected with the following proper name; the genitive 'ردِ افراسیاب' identifies whose hunting-ground it is: 'the hunting-ground (passage) of Afrasiab.' The whole phrase denotes motion toward/arrival at Afrasiab's hunting-ground.
segments.s133-g56: This is a balanced poetic phrase: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of' meaning here 'on (the)'. 'یک دست' literally 'one hand' is an idiom meaning 'on one side'. 'ریگ' is 'sand'. The second half repeats the pattern 'و ز یک دست آب' — 'and on the other side water.' Together: 'with sand on one side and water on the other.'
segments.s133-g1: This is a nominal clause asking 'what did ... say'. چه is an exclamatory/interrogative particle ('what' in an exclamatory sense). آن سراینده مرد دلیر is a noun phrase in apposition: آن ('that/the') + سراینده ('poet') + مرد دلیر ('brave man') — the adjective دلیر follows the noun as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s133-g2: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'that poet' ('who/that'). ناگه is an adverb 'suddenly'. برآویخت is a past tense (poetic past) verb 'rose up/ sprang up / attacked'. با marks the accompanying/instrumental relation ('with' / 'at'); نره شیر is a noun phrase 'male lion' (نره = male + شیر = lion).
segments.s133-g3: که‌گر is a contracted poetic form of 'که اگر' = 'if'. نام مردی literally 'the name of a man' is idiomatic for 'a man's reputation/fame'. بجویی is present subjunctive/imperative second-person form 'you seek' (classical present marked by -ی), and همی is the archaic continuous/habitual marker that follows the verb in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s133-g4: This clause states the manner/means: رخ 'face' is the direct object placed before the verb (poetic word order). تیغ هندی is an instrumental noun phrase 'Indian sword/blade' that supplies the instrument, and بشویی is the present subjunctive/imperative 'you wash' (again with the archaic -ی and the same verb form). The final همی is the continuous/habitual marker; together the phrase means 'you must wash (your) face with the Indian sword' — a figurative way to say one must fight to win fame.
segments.s133-g5: ز (poetic form of از) introduces the object 'from/because of' — here 'from bad things'. بدها is the plural 'bad things/evils'. نبایدت is the impersonal negative 'must not' with the enclitic -ت marking 'you' (literally 'it must not (for you)'). پرهیز is the noun 'avoidance/caution' and کرد is the light verb 'to do' used to form the verbal phrase 'پرهیز کردن' = 'to avoid'. The whole phrase means 'you must not practice avoidance of bad things' (i.e., do not avoid them entirely).
segments.s133-g6: که here gives the explanatory/causal clause 'for/because'. پیش آیدت is a verb phrase meaning 'comes upon you / befalls you' — آیدت is 'it comes to you' with the enclitic -ت. روز ننگ و نبرد is the subject/complement 'a day of shame and battle'. The clause explains the reason you should not avoid (i.e., because such a day may come to you).
segments.s133-g7: زمانه 'the times' or 'fortune' is the subject. چو (classical for 'when') + آمد ('came/comes') form a temporal clause 'when the times come'. بتنگی ('narrowness') is a poetic noun for 'hardship' and فراز ('on high / to a height') intensifies it: together meaning roughly 'when the age reaches hardship' or 'when hardship mounts'.
segments.s133-g8: هم 'even/also' emphasizes the following. از تو 'from you' marks the person affected. نگردد is a present/subjunctive negative of گشت/گردیدن 'to turn/become' meaning 'will not be turned' (here 'will not be able to turn away'). به پرهیز 'to avoidance' (pursing avoidance), باز 'back/again' — idiomatically: 'even your attempt at avoidance will not turn you back' i.e. you cannot escape the hardship. Word order and the negative verb make this a classical, compact statement.
segments.s133-g129: 'شده' is the perfect/passive form of شدن used here as a light verb meaning "have appeared / have become" and introduces the following noun phrase. Word order is verb (شده) + noun phrase (گرد سوار انجمن); 'سوار انجمن' is a noun phrase 'riders/horsemen of the assembly' (possessive/attributive link is implicit in the noun sequence).
segments.s133-g130: 'چنین' ('such') emphasizes the following description; 'نامبردار' here is an adjectival/epithet meaning 'renowned' (not merely 'the aforementioned'), and 'و' simply coordinates that adjective with 'شمشیرزن' ('swordsman'). The three words form a postnominal descriptive phrase modifying the riders just mentioned.
segments.s133-g131: Word order: subject/predicate phrase in compressed, poetic style. 'یکی' = 'one (a single man)' is the subject; 'باشد' is a present/subjunctive/poetic form of 'to be' asserting existence/identity; 'از ما' 'of us' marks membership; 'وزیشان' ('وز' + 'ایشان') here is a nominal phrase meaning 'in their weight / worth' — i.e. 'worth compared to them.' Together the segment says 'one of us, in their worth, (is)...'.
segments.s133-g132: 'سپه' means 'army/host' and heads a question introduced by 'چند' ('how many'); 'باید' is the modal 'must/ought' and governs the infinitival/questional sense of what must be reckoned; 'ز ترکان' = 'from/of the Turks' (poetic 'ز' = از), and 'شمار' is the noun 'number/count'. The whole segment asks, given the previous claim, how many Turkish hosts must be counted.
segments.s133-g133: 'بر' + orthographic 'ین' form 'برین' = 'on this'; the prepositional phrase 'برین دشت' = 'on this plain'. 'اگر' introduces the conditional 'if'. 'ویژه' (= 'special/especially') + 'تنها' ('alone/only') qualify 'منم' ('I am') — the clause states 'if I alone am specially singled out.' Word order is typical poetic inversion (prepositional phrase fronted, then conditional + predicate).
segments.s133-g134: 'که' is the relative/claused linker 'who/that' introducing additional self‑description. 'بر پشت' = 'on the back (of)', followed by 'گلرنگ' (here a name or an epithet for a mount — literally 'flower‑colored/rosy'), and 'در جوشنم' = 'in my cuirass/armor' where 'در' = 'in' and the suffix '-م' on جوشن indicates 'my'. The clause describes the speaker's position/equipment: 'who is on Golrang's back in my armor.'
segments.s133-g135: 'چنو' (variant of 'چنان') + 'کینه' = 'with such hatred / such a grudge'. 'خواهی بیاید' is the future/intentional second‑person construction 'you will come' (خواهی + بیاید), here meaning 'if you come/wish to come with such hate'. 'م' and 'را' in sequence represent the clitic object 'مرا' ('me') split in the manuscript: grammatically they mark the person affected (object) of 'بیاید'.
segments.s133-g136: 'از ایران' = 'from Iran'; 'سپاهی' is 'an army/warriors' (here direct object or scope of reference); 'نباید' is the impersonal/modal negative 'is not necessary / must not / I do not need' in poetic usage; again 'م'+'را' together function as the object clitic 'me' (here emphasized by the following 'را' as the object marker). The segment asserts that, faced with the other's hatred, the speaker does not need an Iranian army.
segments.s133-g33: به مستی is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning “in drunkenness” (به used adverbially). چنین گفت is the verb phrase “thus said” (گفت, past tense), placed before the subject (یک روز گیو); word order here is stylistically inverted — the verb phrase + adverbials precede the temporal phrase and the subject (common in epic Persian). یک روز is a temporal adverbial “one day,” and گیو is the subject (Giv).
segments.s133-g34: به رستم is the indirect-object prepositional phrase “to Rostam.” که‌ای here functions as an archaic/vocative particle (equivalent to که ای / «O…») used to address someone directly; نامبردار is an epithet “renowned” modifying the following noun; نیو appears as a vocative name or nickname and completes the direct address. The whole segment is a direct address attached to the verb in the previous segment.
segments.s133-g35: گر is the conditional conjunction “if.” ایدون is an older/present/future form of آمدن meaning “comes/arrives.” که introduces the noun phrase رای شکار “the counsel/plan of hunting” (رای = plan/counsel; شکار = hunt) as the thing that might occur; آیدت is the verbal form آید + enclitic ـت “to you” meaning “comes to you / befalls you.” The clause sets up a conditional protasis.
segments.s133-g36: چو here = “when / if.” یوز دونده is a noun + participial adjective “the swift-running cheetah” (دونده modifying یوز). به کار آیدت is a standard idiom meaning “be of use to you” (به کار = for use; آیدت = comes to you / is useful to you). This clause gives the consequent or explanatory circumstance tied to the previous conditional.
segments.s133-g37: به introduces a destination “to.” نخچیرگاه is the compound noun “hunting-ground.” رد here functions as a relational/nominal element (literary/archaic) connecting or specifying relation to the following name — essentially pointing to “the hunting-ground (track/passage) of Afrasiab.” افراسیاب is the proper name. The phrase therefore marks the place associated with Afrasiab.
segments.s133-g38: بپوشیم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive (بـ‑ prefix + پوشیم) meaning “let us cover / we will cover.” تابان رخ آفتاب is a noun phrase: تابان “shining” modifies رخ “face,” and آفتاب is in genitive relation with رخ (“face of the sun”); together “the sun’s shining face.” The verb precedes its object (stylistic order).
segments.s133-g39: ز is the poetic preposition “from / of / with” here introducing sources or instruments. گرد سواران functions as a unit meaning “the band / circle of horsemen” (گرد in older texts helps form collective phrases). و از introduces additional sources “and from / and of,” followed by یوز و باز “cheetahs and hawks” (باز = falcon/hawk). The whole segment lists the forces or means involved.
segments.s133-g40: بگیریم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/imperative‑hortative (“let us take / we will seize / secure”) formed with the prefix بـ. آرام روز دراز is the object: آرام “calm/peaceful” modifies روز دراز “long day” (دراز following روز as adjective). The clause states the intended result: to secure a long peaceful day.
segments.s133-g73: که is the subordinating particle 'that' introducing the clause. از ما (from us) marks the source, به افراسیاب is the dative/recipient 'to Afrasiab', and این زمان is a temporal adverbial 'this time' placed at the clause end; the main verb comes in the next segment, so this segment supplies source, recipient and time before the predicate.
segments.s133-g74: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/indeed'. رسید is the perfect/past 3rd-sing. of رسیدن 'reached/arrived' (verb precedes subject here, a common poetic order). آگهی is the subject/object 'news/information' and بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the whole clause.
segments.s133-g75: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one'. چاره سازد is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive or optative 'let him devise/prepare a remedy/plan'. بیاید and بجنگ are jussive/subjunctive verbs 'let him come' and 'fight' — a sequence of jussives giving commands or wishes addressed impersonally to 'یکی'.
segments.s133-g76: کند is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive 'let him do/make' and links back to the prior jussive verbs (i.e., 'let him come and make/perform ...'). دشت نخچیر is the direct object 'the plain of game / hunting-plain', and بر یوز تنگ is a prepositional phrase 'for/against the yūz (hunting animal) narrow' — literally 'make the plain narrow for the yūz.' Word order is verb → object → adjunct (poetic).
segments.s133-g77: بباید is impersonal 'it is necessary/one must'. طلایه is the noun 'vanguard' (thing to be sent). به ره بر is a compound prepositional expression 'to/toward the road' and یکی is the indefinite beneficiary/recipient 'to one (certain man)'. The segment states the necessity of sending a vanguard toward a particular person/place.
segments.s133-g78: که introduces the subordinate clause (purpose/result). چون here means 'when', and آگهی یابد is verb phrase 'he finds/receives the news' (یابد is subjunctive/present 'may find' or simple present in a temporal clause); او is the pronoun 'he' referring back to 'یکی', and اندکی 'a little' indicates limited extent — 'he will have a little (warning/knowledge)'. The verb precedes the pronoun (poetic order).
segments.s133-g79: بیاید is jussive/subjunctive 'let him come', and دهد is 3rd‑person present subjunctive 'let him give' (two verbs forming a coordinated jussive: 'come and give'). آگهی is the object 'information/news' and از سپاه specifies its source 'from/about the army'.
segments.s133-g80: نباید is the impersonal negative 'one must not / it must not be allowed'. که introduces the subordinate clause; گیرد is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive 'let him seize' (or 'that [someone] seize'), بداندیش 'malicious/ill‑minded' modifies the (implied) subject, and راه 'the road/way' is the object — i.e. 'one must not allow a malicious person to seize the road.'
segments.s133-g113: بیامد is a simple past verb 'came' placed before its noun subject (common in Persian poetic inversion). دمان here denotes the place 'the foothill/skirts' and تا ... به نخچیرگاه means 'as far as/to the hunting-ground' with تا used in the sense 'as far as'. The prepositional phrase به نخچیرگاه ('to the hunting-ground') marks the extent reached.
segments.s133-g114: This clause has a slightly archaic, compressed word order: تهمتن (Tahmātan, an epithet for Rostam) is the subject, همی is the old habitual/progressive marker placed before the verb, and خورد is used in an epic sense 'to fall upon/encounter' (here: attack/engage). می‌با is an archaic modal (contracted from می‌باید) meaning 'is about/ought to' or 'is destined to', and سپاه is the object 'the host/army'. Together: 'Tahmātan was (about to) fall upon the host.'
segments.s133-g115: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت here functions as the past verb 'said/spoke' (poetic word order places the verb before the indirect object and the subject), با رستم marks the person addressed ('to/with Rostam'), and شیرمرد is the speaker 'the brave man' placed after the phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s133-g116: که introduces the reported speech/command ('that' / introducing what was said). برخیز is an imperative 'stand up/arise.' و simply links the two imperatives 'and.' از + خرمی means 'from the merriment/joy' (از = 'from'), and بازگرد is an imperative 'return' (here: 'come back' or 'withdraw'). The whole gives two linked commands addressed to Rostam.
segments.s133-g117: که again introduces an explanatory subordinate clause. چندان is a degree adverb 'so much/so great,' placed before the verblike noun سپاهست ('is a host' — fused poetic form). کاندازه نیست is a negative quantifying clause: کانـ (کـ = that) + اندازه ('measure/extent') + نیست ('is not') giving 'that cannot be measured' or 'to such a degree that there is no measure.'
segments.s133-g118: ز is the poetic form of از ('of/among'). لشکر is 'the army/host' and introduces the domain. بلندی و پستی 'high and low' are paired nouns meaning rank or elevation; یکیست ('is one') is a verblike predicate asserting equality — 'high and low are one' among that army. Note the poetic inversion and compact negation/identity structure.
segments.s133-g119: This is a nominal phrase in apposition: درفش 'the banner/standard', جفاپیشه an epithet meaning 'oppression-practicing' or 'oppressor', and افراسیاب the personal name. Together they form 'the banner of Afrasiāb the oppressor.'
segments.s133-g120: همی is the habitual/continuous marker 'continually/still' before the verb. تابد (present classical form of 'تابیدن') means 'shines/gleams.' از گرد here means 'around/about' (from the surrounding area) and introduces the sphere of shining. چون آفتاب is a simile 'like the sun.' The clause says the banner gleams around like the sun.
segments.s133-g105: This chunk is a prepositional phrase + verb phrase: ز هر سو = 'from every side' (ز = poetic 'from', هر سو = 'each/ every side') followed by فرستاد سپاه = 'he sent an army/host'. بی‌مر is an archaic/uncertain modifier placed before سپاه and qualifies the host (literally 'bi-mar' — here translated as 'unending/unremitting' or 'countless'). Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase, verb, then its direct object (سپاه) modified by the preceding adjective.
segments.s133-g106: بدان here is a compound of the preposition ب(ِ) 'to/against' + a demonstrative-like element (here written دان) giving 'to those/against those'; سرکشان is a plural/agentive form (literally 'the headstrong/rebellious ones') and functions as the indirect object. تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that/in order that' and governs the following subjunctive verb بگیرند 'they may seize/take' with راه as its object ('the road' = the route/way).
segments.s133-g107: چو = 'when'; this clause is temporal: 'گرازه چو گرد سپه را بدید' = 'when the sow saw the host around (her)'. گرد here functions as an adverbial/adpositional element meaning 'around/about', and را is the definite object marker attached to سپه. The verb بدید is simple past 'saw'.
segments.s133-g108: This is two coordinated verbal actions: بیامد 'it/they came/approached' followed by سپه را همه بنگرید 'the whole army looked/gazed (upon it)'. سپه را is topicalized by را; همه qualifies the subject/participants ('all'/'everyone'). بنگرید is a present/imperative-form verb in the poem used here as a narrative past sense 'looked' or 'gazed' (poetic usage).
segments.s133-g109: آنک = a relative/demonstrative 'that which' or 'those' and starts a nominal clause: 'بدید آنک شد روی گیتی سیاه' = 'he saw that (it) made the face of the world black' / 'he saw that the world's face became black'. شد is the past of 'become', and روی گیتی = 'the face/surface of the world' (noun + genitive). سیاه modifies روی گیتی as predicate adjective.
segments.s133-g110: A noun chain naming the thing that darkened the world: درفش سپهدار توران سپاه = 'the banner (درفش) of the commander (سپهدار) of Turan's army (توران سپاه)'. Persian here strings nouns in apposition/genitive relation: the first noun is the head (banner) and the following nouns identify whose banner it is (the commander of the Turanian army).
segments.s133-g111: ازانجا 'from there/thereupon' sets the scene; چو = 'when'. باد دمان گشت باز is a simile/description: باد = 'wind', دمان = 'hem/edge (of a garment or cloak)', گشت = 'became/turned' (past), and باز = 'back/again' — together meaning roughly 'when the hem blew back like the wind' or 'when the hem turned back as if by the wind'. Word order is adverbial phrase → verb.
segments.s133-g112: This clause addresses someone (تو 'you') and reports speech/narration: گفتی here functions as the reporting verb 'you said' (poetic form). به زخم اندر آمد گراز is the reported content: به = 'into/toward', زخم = 'wound', اندر = 'in/within', آمد = 'came', گراز = 'the boar'. Word order is typical Persian: (indirect) object/locative phrase before the verb.
segments.s133-g89: ز here is the poetic short form of از meaning 'from' and introduces the source ('from the host'). The object جهان‌دیدگان is marked by را and follows the usual Persian SOV flexibility: 'ز لشکر جهان‌دیدگان را بخواند' — بخواند is 3rd-person past (classical/poetic) meaning 'he called/summoned' (not 'read'), so the clause says 'he summoned the world-seers from the army.'
segments.s133-g90: ز again marks source/association ('of/about Rostam' or 'from Rostam'); بسی is a classical intensifier meaning 'very much'/'many' here; داستانها is the plural 'tales/legends'; براند is a 3rd-person past-form of راند used idiomatically — literally 'he drove/sent forth' but here means 'he produced/related' many tales (i.e., 'he told many tales about Rostam').
segments.s133-g91: وزان is a fused poetic connector (و + ز) meaning 'and from that / and of those'; هفت (seven) is implicit in the phrase as given — گرد here appears in a classical sense tied to a group/compound (part of 'هفت گرد') and stands with سوار 'rider/horseman'; دلیر is an adjective 'brave'. The phrase lists 'those seven brave horsemen.'
segments.s133-g92: که introduces a relative clause 'who'; بودند is the past plural 'they were'; هر یک means 'each one'; به here has the sense 'in/by' forming the idiom به کردار 'in the manner/behaviour of'; کردار is 'conduct/behaviour'; شیر is a metaphorical simile 'lion' — so 'each one behaved like a lion.'
segments.s133-g93: که begins a content clause 'that'; ما را with را marks 'for us' or the party concerned; بباید is the classical impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / we must' (here obligation for the speakers); کنون is the adverb 'now'; ساختن is the infinitive 'to make/arrange/bring about' — together: 'that now it is necessary for us to arrange (this).' Note the archaic modal form بباید (rather than modern باید).
segments.s133-g94: بناگاه is an adverb meaning 'suddenly'; بردن and تاختن are infinitives coordinated here: بردن 'to take/seize' and تاختن 'to gallop/charge'; یکی is the object 'one (of them)'. The cluster expresses the planned actions: 'to seize one suddenly and to charge.'
segments.s133-g95: گراین is the conditional particle + demonstrative (classical 'گر این' = 'if these'); هفت یل 'seven heroes/champions' is the object, marked by را; بچنگ آوریم is a verbal phrase 'to bring into the grasp / to capture' with آوریم first-person plural subjunctive/future sense 'we bring/we can bring' — so the clause reads 'If we capture these seven heroes...' (Note: the provided sense list for گراین does not reflect its conditional use, so sense is marked -1).
segments.s133-g96: جهان is 'the world' (here likely 'the realm / public'); پیش means 'before / in the presence of'; کاووس is the king Kavus; تنگ آوریم is a compound verb 'to make narrow / bring into straits' in 1st-person plural 'we will bring (into difficulty/narrowness)'. Together the clause literally says 'we will bring the world into a tight/narrow state before Kaavus' — an idiomatic threat or promise of making affairs constricted/urgent around the king (i.e., affecting the world as regards Kaavus).
segments.s133-g153: می‌بابلی is a compound noun meaning ‘Babylonian wine’ (noun first); the adjective سرخ follows it and modifies its color. در introduces the locative phrase در جام زرد (‘in/within a yellow cup’), where جام is the noun and زرد is the postpositive adjective describing the cup.
segments.s133-g154: تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (subject or agentive figure). بروی here links Tahmatan to Zowāreh (an archaic/compact phrasing meaning ‘before/on/unto Zowāreh’ in this context); زواره is the personal name receiving the action, and بخورد is the past third‑person form of خوردن used here for ‘he drank’ (classical usage allows خوردن for drinking). The clause thus describes Tahmatan placing/presenting and the drinking that follows.
segments.s133-g155: زواره is the proper name; چو here functions as a simile particle ‘as/like’. بلبل is a simile noun ‘nightingale’ (a poetic image). به کف is a prepositional phrase where به indicates motion/toward/into and کف is ‘palm’, and برنهاد is a past tense verb meaning ‘placed/raised (into)’, so the clause says he, like a nightingale, lifted/placed (it) into his palm.
segments.s133-g156: هم is an adverb meaning ‘also/even’; از introduces the object/relatum (‘of/regarding’); شاه کاووس is the name ‘King Kavus’. کی here is the interrogative/adverb ‘when’; کرد یاد is the compound expression ‘to make remembrance’ (یاد کردن) where کرد is the auxiliary/past verb and یاد is the noun ‘remembrance’ — together meaning ‘remembered’. The whole clause asks rhetorically when he remembered the king.
segments.s133-g157: بخورد here uses classical خوردن in the intransitive/accidental sense (‘fell upon’/‘befell’), followed by و ‘and’ linking the two verbs. ببوسید is past third‑person ‘he kissed’ and روی زمین is a locative phrase with روی meaning ‘upon/on’ (followed by زمین ‘the ground’). Together: ‘he fell and kissed the ground.’
segments.s133-g158: تهمتن is the subject (Tahmatan). برو here functions as a compact narrative particle/marker linking actions (an archaic/formal connective in this context, effectively ‘thereupon/then’), and برگرفت is the past verb ‘he took up/raised (him)’. آفرین is an exclamation or noun ‘praise/bravo’ used as the praise Tahmatan utters or gives. The clause means Tahmatan lifted him and praised him.
segments.s133-g159: که is a subordinating/explanatory particle ‘for/that’. جام is ‘cup’. The reduplicated برادر برادر is an idiomatic repetition meaning ‘one after another’ or ‘cup after cup’ (literally ‘brother by brother’ in older usage), and خورد is the past third‑person of خوردن ‘he drank’. The clause explains that he drank cup after cup.
segments.s133-g160: هژبر is a proper name (Hojabr). آنک is a relative/demonstrative conjunction ‘that/for/which’, introducing a relative clause; او is the third‑person pronoun ‘he’. جام is again ‘cup’, and می‌بشکرد is an imperfective/past habitual classical verb meaning ‘would split/used to split’ (from بشکستن/بشکردن) — i.e. ‘he who used to break/split the cup’. The clause characterises Hojabr as the one who used to smash the cup.
segments.s133-g137: تو‌ای is a poetic vocative 'O you' addressing someone directly. می‌گسار (mey-gasār) is a compound noun 'wine-pourer' or 'cupbearer.' از + می‌بابلی is a prepositional phrase 'from/with Babylonian wine' describing the kind/source of wine; the preposition از here means 'from/out of' in a literal/partitive sense.
segments.s133-g138: بپیمای is an imperative/imperatival form (ب‑ prefix + present stem) meaning 'measure out / serve / pour' (a command to the cupbearer). تا functions as 'until' and introduces a limit of extent; سر here means 'head' (physical top) used in a partitive/measure phrase. یکی is the indefinite 'a certain/one', and بلبلی qualifies it as 'a nightingale (‑like / of a nightingale)'; together تا سر یکی بلبلی = 'up to the head of a certain nightingale.'
segments.s133-g139: بپیمود is a past-tense verb 'he filled/fulfilled/measured' (poetic past). The following noun می‌ساقی is the agent 'the cupbearer' — the line uses a somewhat inverted, poetic word order (verb before its noun). و is the coordinating 'and/then.' داد here is a past verb 'gave' (he gave), and زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying داد.
segments.s133-g140: تهمتن شد is 'Tahmatan became' (اسمِ شخص + شد as a change‑of‑state verb). از introduces the cause/reason ('because of' / 'from'), and دادنش is the masdar/gerund + pronominal suffix meaning 'his giving' (داد + -ش 'his/it'). شاد is the adjective 'glad' and زود is an adverb 'soon' — together: 'Tahmatan soon became glad because of his giving.'
segments.s133-g141: به + کف + بر + نهاد is a fixed poetic sequence meaning 'he placed upon (into) the palm': به (towards/into) + کف (palm) + بر (on/upon) + نهاد (past of نهادن, 'placed'). آن درخشنده جام is the demonstrative + adjective + noun 'that shining cup,' the direct object of نهاد.
segments.s133-g142: نخستین is 'the first.' ز کاووس is the poetic genitive 'of/from Kavus.' کی is a relative/interrogative used here as a relative 'which/that.' برد نام is a verbal phrase 'bore the name' (برد is the past of بردن 'to bear/carry' used with نام 'name' to mean 'to bear a name' i.e. 'to bear the name of'). The whole segment describes the cup as 'the first that bore Kavus's name.'
segments.s133-g143: که here introduces an optative/wishing clause ('that' / 'may it be that'). شاه زمانه is a genitive phrase 'the king of the age' (i.e. the sovereign of the time). مرا + یاد باد is a conventional older Persian formula meaning 'may (he) be remembered by me' — مَرا (here split in the text as م + را) functions as the experiencer/agent in an older syntax and باد is the optative particle 'may.'
segments.s133-g144: همیشه is the adverb 'always.' بروبومش (بروبوم + -ش) means 'his land / his dwelling' with the possessive suffix -ش 'his.' آباد is an adjective 'prosperous/inhabited,' and باد is the optative 'may' placed at the end to express the wish: 'may his land always be prosperous.'
segments.s133-g65: ز خنده is a poetic prepositional phrase (ز = 'from/because of') modifying the following clause; نیاسود is a past negative verb meaning 'did not rest/cease' and is placed before its noun (لب) by poetic inversion, so the literal order is 'from laughter did not rest the lip' meaning 'the lip did not cease from laughter for a moment.' زمان (یک زمان) gives the duration 'for a moment.'
segments.s133-g66: ببودند is an archaic past plural form of بودن ('they were'); it agrees with the understood plural subject (people, or collectively the lips/companions) and links back to the preceding temporal phrase. روشن‌دل و شادمان are two adjectival predicates joined by و describing their state: 'bright‑hearted and happy.'
segments.s133-g67: به یک هفته gives a duration ('for one week'); زین‌گونه ('in this way/thus') characterises that period. با + می + بدست is a compact/poetic grouping meaning 'with wine in hand' or 'with wine to hand' (با = 'with', می = 'wine', بدست acting as 'in hand/by means'), describing the accompaniment or manner during that week.
segments.s133-g68: گهی ... گه marks alternating actions ('sometimes ... sometimes ...'). تاختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'galloping/charging' used with گهی to mean 'sometimes galloping'; نشست is a past tense 'sat/settled' (here meaning they 'sat' or 'rested in joy') and نشاط ('delight') is the state in which they sat. The phrase therefore expresses alternating behaviour: sometimes they rode fast, sometimes they sat joyous.
segments.s133-g69: بهشتم is a prepositional/locative phrase 'to my presence/my court' (به = 'to/for'); تهمتن is the proper name (Tahmatan); بیامد is past simple 'came'; and پگاه is an adverbial of time 'at dawn/early morning.' The order places the time and destination around the verb in a poetic arrangement but the basic structure is 'Tahmatan came to my court at dawn.'
segments.s133-g70: رای شایسته زد is a common collocation meaning 'he proposed/offered a fitting plan/opinion' (رای = 'plan, counsel'; شایسته = 'appropriate/fitting'; رای زدن = 'to give a counsel/plan'). با سپاه is the prepositional phrase ('with the army') stating the content/means of that plan — i.e. 'to strike/attack with the army.' Here زد functions as the verb of expression ('uttered/proposed') and also implies the action envisaged by the counsel.
segments.s133-g71: چنین گفت رستم is an inverted order meaning 'Rostam said thus' (چنین = 'thus'; گفت = 'said' here; رستم the speaker). بدان is the prepositional sequence ('to those' — ب = to/into + دان as an archaic/demonstrative element), and سرکشان is a plural epithet 'the rebellious/proud ones' addressed by Rostam. The verb گفت precedes the subject for poetic emphasis.
segments.s133-g72: بدان again marks 'to those' (preposition + demonstrative) and is followed by two appositive epithets describing the addressees: گرزداران ('mace‑bearers' — people bearing maces) and مردم‌کشان ('people‑slayers'/'those who kill men'). Grammatically these are plural noun phrases in apposition, intensifying the reproach/description.
segments.s133-g41: 'به' is the preposition 'to/towards' placed before the target; 'گور' here is the wild ass/onager (a typical hunting quarry in epic poetry); 'تگاور' is a rider/warrior modifying that animal (the riders' onagers). 'کمند' is the hunting noose/lasso, and 'افگنیم' is an archaic 1st-person-plural verbal form (from افگندن) meaning 'we cast/throw' (optative/jussive nuance 'let us cast' or simple 'we cast') that closes the clause. Word order is preposition + object + modifier + object (کمند) + verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s133-g42: The clause begins with instrumental 'به' used as 'by/with' (here marking the instrument), followed by 'شمشیر' (sword). 'بر' = 'upon/against' governs 'شیر' (lion). 'بند' = 'fetter/bond' is the object, and 'افگنیم' repeats the same archaic 1pl verb 'we cast/place', so: 'with the sword we place a fetter upon the lion.' The parallel repetition of افگنیم links the two balanced actions in the verse.
segments.s133-g43: This phrase contains the demonstrative idea 'to that (place) of Turan' followed by the verbal phrase. In poetic spelling the idea 'به آن' appears as the cluster here (splitted in the provided tokens), then 'دشت توران' = 'the plain of Turan.' 'شکاری کنیم' is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive from کردن with 'شکاری' (hunting) as the object: 'let us go hunting' or 'we shall hunt.' The verb carries a hortative/intentional sense ('let us ...' / 'we will ...').
segments.s133-g44: 'که' introduces a purposive/subordinate clause ('so that' / 'that'), 'اندر جهان' = 'in the world,' and 'یادگاری کنیم' = 'we make/leave a memorial' (یادگاری here = a lasting sign/inscription/memento, and کنيم = 'we do/make'). The whole clause expresses purpose/result: they hunt in order to leave something remembered in the world.
segments.s133-g45: 'بدو' = 'to him' (an enclitic prepositional phrase placed before the verb in poetry), followed by گفت which here functions as the past 'said' (though the provided sense list mislabels it as 'conversation'). 'رستم' is the speaker. 'که' begins the reported clause, and 'بی‌کام تو' literally 'your being without success' is the predicate of the reported wish—so this segment introduces Rostam's speech expressing concern that the addressee not be unsuccessful.
segments.s133-g46: 'مبادا' is a negative optative/precautionary particle meaning 'may it not be'/'lest'; 'گذر' here names the passing (i.e. the passage of life or death), 'تا' = 'until', and 'سرانجام تو' = 'your end/outcome.' Together the clause expresses a wish: 'May it not be that your life passes (i.e. you die) before your aim is achieved' — condensed poetically as 'may you not pass away unfulfilled.'
segments.s133-g47: 'سحرگه' is an adverbial phrase 'at dawn.' The following token cluster corresponds to the demonstrative/location 'to that' (poetic contraction of 'به آن') and 'دشت توران' = 'the plain of Turan.' 'شویم' is the 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/indicative of شدن used here as 'we shall go/become (there)' — together: 'At dawn we shall go into that plain of Turan.'
segments.s133-g48: 'ز' and 'از' are poetic prepositions 'from/of'; 'نخچیر' = game/prey and 'تاختن' = to gallop/charge. The verb 'نغنویم' comes from an old root meaning 'to fall asleep/abate' used here in the negative sense 'we shall not cease/rest.' So the clause says they will not desist from game nor from charging—i.e. they will keep up the hunt and the cavalry charge.
segments.s133-g81: This clause describes placing the projectile before the action: گرازه is the thing being placed, followed by two locative markers (به + زه and بر) and the verb participle نهاده. نهاده is a past participle/verb 'placed/put' that takes its complements before it; به here means 'towards/onto' (placing the arrow onto the string) and بر means 'on/upon' specifying the surface (the bow). Word order (object/place before verb) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s133-g82: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' (i.e., 'he turned to / proceeded with'), and بران refers back to 'that' action ('to/onto that'). کار here = 'task/affair' and بسته is adjectival/participial meaning 'bound/tied' or 'drawn tight'; میان = 'in the middle' or 'between'. Together the clause says he proceeded with that action and the work was held/secured in the middle; the sequence is characteristic of narrative past in Ferdowsi.
segments.s133-g83: سپه را is topicalized (را marking the noun as the definite/topic). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/for', and چون here functions as 'since/when' ('because/when he was...'). او is the pronoun 'he', نگهدار is a noun 'guardian/keeper', and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause therefore means 'since/when the army had him as its guardian'.
segments.s133-g84: همه چاره is the subject phrase 'all (the) means/remedies', دشمنان modifies چاره as 'of the enemies', and خوار بود is a predicate 'were worthless/despised' (خوار describes the worthlessness or humiliation of those remedies). The clause states that every expedient of the enemies amounted to nothing.
segments.s133-g85: This is an idiomatic expression: به + [nouns] + نهادند + روی = 'they set their faces toward ...' Here به marks direction ('toward hunting and eating'), نخچیر and خوردن are the two activities ('game/spoil' and 'eating'), نهادند is the past verb 'they set/placed', and روی ('face') is the object used with نهادن to mean 'turned toward'.
segments.s133-g86: نکردند is the negated past 'they did not do/perform'; کس here functions as an indefinite 'anyone/no one' depending on negation (with نکردند it yields 'no one'), یاد = 'memory/thought/consideration' and پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'aggressive/quarrelsome'. The clause therefore means 'no one remembered/considered aggression' or 'no one thought to attack'. Word order (verb before subject/object) is common in older narrative Persian.
segments.s133-g87: پس is a temporal/continuative adverb 'then', آگاهی is 'information/news', آمد is the past verb 'came', and به + افراسیاب marks the indirect object 'to Afrasiab'. The clause is a straightforward past passive‑type construction: 'news came to Afrasiab.'
segments.s133-g88: ازیشان = از + ایشان 'from them'; شب تیره هنگام خواب is a temporal adverbial phrase 'in the dark night-time during sleep' (تیره = 'dark/ominous', هنگام = 'at the time', خواب = 'sleep' or 'sleeping'). The whole phrase qualifies when and from whom the news arrived: 'from them at the dark hour of night while asleep.'
segments.s133-g121: چو is the subordinating particle 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بشنید is a past-tense verb 'he heard' placed before its subject (poetic inversion); رستم is the subject that follows. بخندید is another past-tense verb 'laughed' and سخت is an adverb modifying it ('strongly/heartily').
segments.s133-g122: بدو is the clitic-like prepositional phrase 'to him'. گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (the provided senses lack the verbal sense, so none match exactly). با ماست is a predicate: با = 'with', ما = 'us', and ست is the contracted copula (ماست = 'is with us'). پیروز بخت is a nominal phrase 'victorious fortune' serving as the predicate complement.
segments.s133-g123: تو is the 2nd‑singular subject 'you'. از here means 'of/regarding' (introducing the thing feared): از شاه ترکان = 'the king of the Turks'. چه ترسی is an interrogative phrase 'what do you fear' where ترسی is the present 2nd‑singular form 'you fear' (classical/poetic form). چنین is an adverb 'so/thus' intensifying the question.
segments.s133-g124: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. گرد here is a poetic noun meaning 'array/host' (no matching sense in the provided list), forming the noun phrase گرد سواران = 'the host/array of horsemen'. سواران is the plural 'horsemen', and توران زمین is a genitive sequence 'the land of Turan' (modifier after the head in poetic word order).
segments.s133-g125: سپاهش is سپاه + the possessive/agentive suffix ش ('his/its army'). فزون نیست is the negated comparative 'is not more/exceeds not', followed by the comparator از + number: از صدهزار = 'than a hundred thousand' (classical order places the predicate before the comparator).
segments.s133-g126: This is a string of epithets modifying سوار ('horsemen'). عنان + پیچ forms عنان‌پیچ 'reins‑twisting' (a compound participial/attributive idea); و is the coordinating 'and'; برگستوان‌ور is a poetic adjective 'with strong arms / mighty‑armed' modifying سوار. The whole phrase describes the kind of horsemen counted above.
segments.s133-g127: بدین دشت is a locative phrase 'in/on this plain' (بدین = 'in this/with this' in classical usage). کین‌برگر is a compound meaning 'avenger' or 'one who returns revenge' (برگر here has an archaic sense 'again/anew' forming the agentive idea), followed by از ما 'of us' and یکی‌ست 'is one' (predicate 'is one of us'). Note classical compound spacing and poetic word order.
segments.s133-g128: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here ('indeed/just'); جنگ ترکان is the noun phrase 'the war of the Turks'; بچشم is a fixed classical locution 'to the eye / in sight of' (literally 'to the eye'), and اندکی‌ست is the predicate 'is small/insignificant.' Together: 'the Turks' war is small to behold.'
segments.s133-g145: ازان پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'thereupon/after that.' The subject تهمتن (Tahmatan, a heroic epithet) follows it. زمین داد بوس is a small, slightly inverted idiom: داد (gave) + بوس (a kiss, noun) with زمین as the indirect object — literally 'gave the earth a kiss,' i.e. 'kissed the ground.' The verb (داد) is past tense.
segments.s133-g146: چنین گفت is a conventional formula meaning 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت here introduces speech). کاین is the archaic fused form of که + این, introducing the content of what was said ('that this...'). باده بر یاد طوس is a nominal phrase: باده (wine) + بر (on/for) + یاد (remembrance) + طوس (Tūs, the hero) — 'the wine is for the remembrance of Tūs.' There is no copula; the whole noun phrase functions as the quoted content.
segments.s133-g147: سران جهاندار is a nominal phrase: سران (the chiefs/leaders) modified by جهاندار ('world‑holding' / sovereign), followed by the past plural verb برخاستند 'they rose (up)'. Word order is noun + modifier + verb, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s133-g148: ابا here is an older/poetic particle expressing reluctance or a humble tone toward the addressee; the clause means roughly 'they, with reluctance/respect, made a request to the champion.' پهلوان is the addressee ('the champion'), خواهش is 'request/desire', and آراستند (past plural) means 'they fashioned/expressed' (here: 'made' or 'voiced' a request).
segments.s133-g149: که introduces reported speech/explanation: 'that...' ما را uses را after ما as a dative/ethical object marker ('for us'); بدین جام is an adverbial/locative phrase 'with this cup' (بدین = 'with/by this', جام = 'cup'). می‌جای نیست is the predicate: می‌جای (befit/take the place) + نیست 'is not' → 'is not fitting/appropriate.'
segments.s133-g150: به می‌با تو is an instrumental/temporal frame + modal: به (by/with) + می‌با (archaic modal 'must/ought') + تو (you). ابلیس را پای نیست is a nominal clause with را marking ابلیس (the devil) as the topical/affected entity; پای نیست literally 'has no foot' = 'has no foothold.' The whole clause warns 'you must not give the devil a foothold (by wine).' Note the poetic inversion and the idiomatic پای نیست.
segments.s133-g151: This is an asyndetic list of direct objects: می‌و (fruit/poetic for wine), گرز (mace), یک زخم ('one wound' — numeric + noun), و میدان جنگ ('and the field of war'). These items are the objects referred to by the following clause; the list lacks an explicit verb here and is completed by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s133-g152: جز از تو کسی را نیامد به چنگ is a negative-existential statement with fronted exception: جز از تو ('except from you') + کسی را ('no one' with را used for emphasis/topic) + نیامد (did not come) + به چنگ (into the grasp). Together: 'No one, except you, came into the grasp' — an idiom meaning 'none but you seized/possessed them.' نیامد is past tense negative and به چنگ آمدن is the idiom 'to come into the grip/seize.'
segments.s133-g97: بکردار is an archaic/poetic spelling (= به کردار) meaning “in the manner of”; نخچیر is the object meaning “prey / spoil”; باید is a modal verb “must / ought to” that governs the following verbal idea; شدن here functions as an infinitival/auxiliary notion (“to become / to be done”) so the whole chunk expresses necessity (‘must, in the manner of hunters, be made/taken as prey’). Word order places the manner phrase before the modal + infinitive.
segments.s133-g98: بناگاه is an adverb “suddenly” placed before the clause; لشکر is the grammatical subject “the army/host”; برایشان is برای + شان where برای carries the sense “against / directed at” here (targeting them) and شان is the third‑person plural possessive/target “them”; زدن is the verb “to strike/attack.” Together the segment says that the army should suddenly strike at them (the verb appears at the end, as is normal in Persian).
segments.s133-g99: گزین کرد is a past tense verbal phrase (‘he chose’), where گزین (select) plus کرد (the light verb ‘did / made’) yields ‘selected/chose.’ شمشیر زن سی‌هزار is a noun phrase ‘swordsmen, thirty thousand’ (classical word order allows the numeral after the noun), so the whole segment reads ‘he chose thirty thousand swordsmen.’
segments.s133-g100: همه qualifies the chosen group (‘all’); رزمجو is an appositive/descriptor meaning ‘warrior’; از در کارزار is a prepositional phrase literally ‘from in the battlefield’ (an idiomatic classical phrasing meaning ‘from the ranks/on the field of battle’). The segment as a whole specifies that these thirty thousand were all battle-hardened warriors.
segments.s133-g101: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase ‘thus said’ (چنین = ‘thus/so’ + گفت = ‘said’). با نامداران جنگ is the indirect object phrase ‘to/with the famed ones of war’ (با here marks the addressees, ‘to the renowned warriors’). Word order places the reportative verb before the addressee phrase in this poetic line.
segments.s133-g102: که introduces reported content (‘that’); ما را uses را as an object/emphatic marker with ما (‘we/us’); کنون نیست جای درنگ is a conventional negated existential clause literally ‘now there is not place for delay’ — idiomatically ‘now there is no time to delay.’ Note the negation نیست governs جای (‘place/time’) plus the noun درنگ (‘delay’).
segments.s133-g103: به راه بیابان is a directional prepositional phrase ‘onto/toward the desert road’ (به = ‘to/toward’, راه = ‘road/way’, بیابان = ‘desert’); برون تاختند is the verb phrase ‘they rushed out’ (برون = ‘out/forth’, تاختند = past plural ‘they rushed/charged’). The verb at the end marks past, third‑person plural action.
segments.s133-g104: همه marks the subject ‘all’; جنگ را is the definite object marked with را (‘the war’); گردن افراختند is a compound verb phrase literally ‘they raised the neck’ — a poetic idiom meaning they lifted/advanced the war (i.e., set themselves proudly to battle). The past plural verb افراختند closes the clause.
